text: The impact of evaporation of ammonium nitrate on the closure between hygroscopicity parameter derived from hygroscopic growth at 90% relative humidity, cloud condensation nuclei activity and size-resolved chemical composition measurements for particles in the Thüringer Wald mid-level mountain range in central Germany during fall season 2010 Abstract. Simultaneous measurements of particle hygroscopic growth at 90% relative humidity (RH), cloud condensation nuclei (CCN) activity, and size-resolved chemical composition were carried out in the Thüringer Wald mid-level mountain range in central Germany in the fall of 2010. The impact of evaporation of ammonium nitrate on the closure between hygroscopicity parameter derived from particle hygroscopic growth at 90% RH and CCN activity, and between the derived hygroscopicity parameter and the size-resolved chemical composition measurements was investigated. The median values of the hygroscopicity parameter, κ, for particles of diameters 50, 75, 100, 150, 200, and 250 nm derived from the hygroscopicity measurements were 0.14, 0.14, 0.17, 0.21, 0.24, and 0.28, respectively. The closure between HTDMA-measured (κHTDMA) and chemical composition-derived (κchem) hygroscopicity parameters was carried out based on the Zdanovskii–Stokes–Robinson (ZSR) mixing rule. Using size-averaged chemical composition, the κ values were substantially overpredicted (30 and 40% for 150 and 100 nm particles). Introducing size-resolved chemical composition, however, substantially improved the closure between the measured and predicted κ values. The evaporation of NH4NO3 in the HTDMA system is considered to be a possible explanation for the observed discrepancy. Additionally, the hygroscopic parameter of the organic fraction, κorg, is found to be positively correlated with the O : C ratio (κorg = 0.19 × (O : C) − 0.03). The derived κ values from CCN measurement were around 30% (varied with particle diameters) higher than those derived from particle hygroscopic growth measurements (considering only the hydrophilic mode). The difference between the two κ values might be attributed to surface tension effects, solution non-ideality, gas-particle partitioning of semivolatile compounds, and the partial solubility of constituents or non-dissolved particle matter. Finally, the closure study between CCN-measured (κCCN) and composition-derived (κCCN, chem) hygroscopicity parameters was performed using CCN-derived κ values for individual components. The κCCN values can be well predicted using particle size-resolved chemical composition and the ZSR mixing rule.
explanation: The text uses authentic domain-specific terminology and precise results typical of scientific writing, which could be human-authored; the highly formulaic structure and lack of external references also resemble AI-generated abstracts.
